author: @marklewus5468
text: What a great project! The breakdown voltage of room air is about 3 kv/mm. So you had about 30 kilovolts across that 1 cm gap. Better get thicker gloves :)

author: @Graham_Wideman
text: Looking good!

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: It's beautiful.. I hope it'll work very well. Take care all insulators in it are high impedance. I'm sure you have that covered  .... It's performance might depend on air humidity, it will work far better in dry conditions.

author: @captainprototype187
text:
I am making a 1 foot lathe . I have found that thick and even very thin elastic bands give all the torque that you need. I was very surprised about the thin elastic bands! Also, I picked up a  hairband made out of cloth and elastiic
off he street the other day. It works very well too. I didnt think of the leather straps myself. A rubber bicycle tire can/has to be sown too, I think. thanks, for the ideas!

author: @Alexander_Sannikov
text: keep in mind that when the machine works, sectors on opposite discs attract and it becomes significantly harder to spin. there's a chance there would not be enough friction between leather belt and a lacquered pulley.

author: @InterdimensionalWiz
text:
Ive made whimshurst machines.... Delightful!
I read in a science textbook that their function is not fully understood even today!   ...however that was 1955!     but even so...... fascinating! 
 I think is is a series of 'Electrophorus' all being touched and seperated by the wipers. Hey man... its all free energy man!       mmmmmm...perhaps not but pretty close!

author: @AdricM
text: it does look lovely.   dad made one with us when i was a kid,  his used records for the wheels strips of brass tape and he made his own brushes.   his only had a spark gap and no liden jars,  but we did have a small liden jar to charge up and run around the house zapping each other with.

author: @ianfisher6561
text:
Another fantastic build. Thank you.
All my life I have had an interest in high voltage. Many years ago, before the internet, I purchased a book by R. A. Ford, called Homemade Lightening. If I remember correctly, in his book, Ford used wood coated with graphite as the conductors. I am intrigued by your use of a similar technique, but different role. This comment is NOT meant as a criticism. I will probably be building one very similar to yours. Seems like Ford's book is still available on Amazon.

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text:
yeah, you just need to be careful not to produce  Lichtenberg fractal conductive carbon to graphite channels 
(unless thats what you want oc, for quick and simple fractal channels to get increased internal carbon area in a plate/sheet, burned sugar dipped toast etc) its your 'fractal choice'

text: I have used vacuum tubing meant for automotive use for belts in my Windhurst machine. It is very durable. I joined the two ends with a short piece of rod that goes in the middle of the tubing and is super glued in

author: @stevenw4549
text:
Nicely done. Always wanted to make one of those. I did make a Vandegraph  Generator. Cut strips of Latex gloves for the belt. Used an old record player motor. A pan for the base, some PVC pipe and the old Tribo chart to determine what to make the spools out of. And a modern looking lamp head for the big ball. That's always the hard part finding large metal hollow spheres.
Fascinating effects. I remember reading a story years ago of a maintenance man sent to fix a problem at a factory that made adhesive tape I think it was. There was sheets of material running on rollers from one spool to another and it would go up and the over creating a sort of doorway so you could access the other side. They found while the machine was running you could not walk through the opening unless you turned sideways. Some sort of forcefield seemed to be at play. The maintenance man said he didn't know whether to fix it or sell tickets  LOL. Keep on having fun.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - that is a fascinating story - cheers mate

author: @jeffreyrood8755

I'm looking forward to each step. Great Job!!!
